title: The Valonqar
description: A prophecy that Cersei will be killed by the valonqar — High Valyrian for 'little brother' — who will wrap his hands about her pale white throat and choke the life from her.
notes: Valonqar is High Valyrian for 'little brother.' Most assume Tyrion, but Jaime — born moments after Cersei — also qualifies. Some theorists suggest any younger sibling could fit.

title: Maggy's Three Children
description: Maggy the Frog foretells that Cersei will have three children, and the king will have twenty. Gold shall be their crowns and gold their shrouds, indicating all three of Cersei's children will die.
fulfillment_evidence: Joffrey dies at his wedding feast (Purple Wedding). Myrcella and Tommen are still alive at the end of ADWD, but the prophecy strongly implies all three will die.
notes: The 'gold crowns' refers both to their golden Lannister hair and their royal status. The 'gold shrouds' predicts their deaths.

title: The Younger and More Beautiful Queen
description: Maggy prophesies that a younger, more beautiful queen will come to cast Cersei down and take all that she holds dear.
notes: Cersei fixates on Margaery Tyrell as the younger queen, but Daenerys Targaryen and even Sansa Stark have been proposed as candidates.

title: Azor Ahai Reborn
description: An ancient prophecy that a hero will be reborn to fight the darkness, wielding a burning sword called Lightbringer. The prince that was promised shall draw Lightbringer from the fire.
notes: Melisandre initially identifies Stannis as Azor Ahai reborn, but her visions increasingly point to Jon Snow. Maester Aemon suggests Daenerys may be the prince that was promised, noting the Valyrian word for 'prince' is gender-neutral.

title: Quaithe's Warning to Daenerys
description: Quaithe warns Daenerys that to go north, she must journey south; to reach the west, she must go east; to go forward, she must go back; and to touch the light, she must pass beneath the shadow.
notes: The 'shadow' likely refers to Asshai-by-the-Shadow. This prophecy suggests Daenerys's path to Westeros will be indirect and counterintuitive.

title: Quaithe's Visitor Warning
description: Quaithe warns Daenerys of those who come to her: the sun's son, the mummer's dragon, the lion, the griffin, the kraken, and the dark flame. She tells Daenerys to trust none of them.
fulfillment_evidence: Quentyn Martell (sun's son) arrives and dies. Jon Connington (the griffin) invades with Young Griff. Victarion Greyjoy (the kraken) sails for Meereen. Tyrion (the lion) reaches Meereen.
notes: The 'mummer's dragon' is widely interpreted as Young Griff/Aegon, suggesting he is a false Targaryen — a puppet of Varys (the mummer). The 'dark flame' likely refers to Moqorro.

title: The House of the Undying: The Red Wedding
description: In the House of the Undying, Daenerys sees a vision of a feast where a dead man with a wolf's head sits at a table, presiding over a slaughter with a woman's body floating in water nearby.
fulfillment_evidence: Robb Stark is killed at the Red Wedding. His direwolf Grey Wind's head is sewn onto his body. Catelyn Stark's throat is cut and her body thrown in the river.
notes: One of the most clearly fulfilled visions from the House of the Undying sequence.

title: The House of the Undying: The Blue Flower in the Wall
description: Daenerys sees a blue flower growing from a chink in a wall of ice, filling the air with sweetness.
notes: The blue winter rose is associated with Lyanna Stark. This vision is widely interpreted as pointing to Jon Snow at the Wall, potentially connecting to his Targaryen parentage (R+L=J).

title: The House of the Undying: The Cloth Dragon
description: Daenerys sees a cloth dragon swaying on poles amidst a cheering crowd, suggesting a false dragon propped up by others.
fulfillment_evidence: Young Griff, claiming to be Aegon Targaryen, is supported by Varys and Jon Connington. If he is a Blackfyre or impostor, he is literally a 'cloth dragon' — a false Targaryen on display.
notes: Connects directly to Quaithe's 'mummer's dragon' warning. The 'poles' suggest a puppet or prop, reinforcing the interpretation that Young Griff is not a real Targaryen.

title: The House of the Undying: Daughter of Death
description: The Undying declare Daenerys to be the 'daughter of death,' the 'slayer of lies,' and the 'bride of fire,' each title accompanied by three visions representing three treasons, three mounts, and three fires she must endure.
fulfillment_evidence: Some of the three treasons may have occurred (Mirri Maz Duur for blood, possibly Brown Ben Plumm for gold). Mounts: the silver horse (to love), Drogon (to dread). The third treason and third mount remain unfulfilled.
notes: A sweeping meta-prophecy that frames Daenerys's entire arc. The three categories (death/lies/fire) may correspond to past, present, and future.

title: Ghost of High Heart: The Red Wedding
description: The Ghost of High Heart sees a vision of a woman at a feast with a fish-shaped crown, her hair hanging down her face. She weeps for her lost children.
fulfillment_evidence: Catelyn Tully Stark is a Tully (fish sigil). She is killed at the Red Wedding feast. She clawed her own face in grief after Robb's death before her throat was cut.
notes: The Ghost of High Heart's visions are delivered as songs or dreams. She asks for a song from Tom of Sevenstreams as payment.

title: Ghost of High Heart: The Purple Wedding
description: The Ghost of High Heart sees a maid at a feast with purple serpents in her hair, venom dripping from their fangs.
fulfillment_evidence: Sansa wears a silver hairnet with dark purple amethysts to Joffrey's wedding feast. The amethysts contain the strangler poison used to kill Joffrey — the 'purple serpents' with venom.
notes: Sansa is an unwitting participant. Littlefinger and Olenna Tyrell orchestrated the poisoning using the stones in Sansa's hairnet.

title: Patchface: Under the Sea
description: Patchface sings that under the sea, the birds have scales for feathers and the fish take wing. He says the dead outnumber the living and the mermaids blow their horns of seashell.
notes: Patchface drowned during a shipwreck and came back changed. Melisandre is deeply unsettled by him, seeing visions of skulls around him. His songs may foretell events connected to the Drowned God or death.

title: Patchface: The Shadows Come to Dance
description: Patchface sings that the shadows come to dance and to stay — the shadows come to play. He claims it is always summer under the sea.
notes: The 'shadows' likely reference Melisandre's shadow assassins. Patchface may be foreshadowing more shadow magic or the coming of the Others.

title: Jojen's Greendream: The Sea at Winterfell
description: Jojen dreams that the sea comes to Winterfell and drowns the inhabitants. He sees the waters rush through the halls and the bodies of the dead floating in the flooded castle.
fulfillment_evidence: Theon Greyjoy (an Ironborn, whose sigil is the kraken — a sea creature) captures Winterfell with his men. Many inhabitants are killed. The 'sea' is metaphorical — the Ironborn represent the sea.
notes: Greendreams are prophetic but often metaphorical. Jojen warns Bran but cannot change the outcome — greendreams show what will happen, not what might.

title: Jojen's Greendream: The Chained Wolf
description: Jojen tells Bran he dreamed of a winged wolf bound to earth with grey stone chains, unable to fly. Only when the chains are broken can the wolf take flight.
fulfillment_evidence: The winged wolf is Bran, who has the gift of greensight (warging/flying). The stone chains represent his ignorance of his powers or his confinement in Winterfell. He eventually unlocks his abilities through training with the Three-Eyed Crow.
notes: This dream motivates the Reeds' entire journey north with Bran to find the Three-Eyed Crow.

title: Melisandre's Vision: Snow at the Wall
description: Melisandre looks into her flames seeking Azor Ahai but sees only Snow — a vision that keeps returning despite her attempts to see Stannis.
notes: The capitalized 'Snow' is a deliberate double meaning — the weather and Jon Snow. Melisandre interprets the vision as a warning about the weather but may be missing that Jon is Azor Ahai.

title: Melisandre's Vision: Girl on a Dying Horse
description: Melisandre sees a grey girl on a dying horse fleeing toward the Wall, believing it to be Arya Stark fleeing her marriage to Ramsay Bolton.
fulfillment_evidence: The grey girl turns out to be Alys Karstark, not Arya Stark. Alys arrives at the Wall fleeing a forced marriage. Melisandre's interpretation was wrong even though the vision itself was accurate.
notes: A key example of how Melisandre's visions are genuine but her interpretations are frequently wrong. The flames show truth, but the reader (and Melisandre) must interpret correctly.

title: The Red Comet
description: A red comet appears in the sky at the start of the War of the Five Kings. Multiple characters interpret it differently — as a sign of Lannister triumph, Stannis's kingship, dragons, or old gods' bleeding.
notes: Everyone claims the comet as their sign. Daenerys sees it when her dragons are born. Osha calls it 'blood and fire.' The comet functions as a Rorschach test for each character's worldview.

title: The Direwolf Litter
description: A dead direwolf mother is found with five pups and a sixth (an albino) found separately. The dead stag's antler is lodged in the mother's throat.
fulfillment_evidence: Five pups for five legitimate Stark children, plus Ghost (the albino) for Jon Snow the outsider. The stag killing the direwolf foreshadows the Baratheon-Stark conflict — Robert's visit brings ruin to the Starks. Ned (the direwolf) is killed by the crown (the stag).
notes: Not a spoken prophecy but narrative foreshadowing. The dead mother with an antler in her throat perfectly mirrors the Stark-Baratheon tragedy to come.

title: Daenerys's Dragon Dreams
description: Throughout AGOT, Daenerys has recurring dreams of waking the dragon — she dreams of fire that does not burn, of dragon eggs cracking, and of a great beast emerging from stone.
fulfillment_evidence: Daenerys walks into Khal Drogo's funeral pyre and emerges unburned with three hatched dragons. The stone eggs crack in the fire exactly as her dreams foretold.
notes: These dreams serve as both prophecy and instinct — Daenerys seems to know subconsciously that she can hatch the eggs through fire and sacrifice.

title: Jaime's Weirwood Dream
description: In a dream while sleeping on a weirwood stump, Jaime finds himself in the dark caverns beneath Casterly Rock with Brienne. Their swords glow with pale flame. Cersei and the ghosts of past Kingsguard appear, and the flames begin to die as Cersei leaves him behind.
notes: The weirwood connection suggests this is a true prophetic dream. The flaming swords echo Lightbringer. Jaime wakes and immediately rides back to save Brienne from the bear pit — the dream seems to guide his choices.

title: The Prince That Was Promised — Rhaegar's Belief
description: Rhaegar Targaryen believed his son Aegon was the prince that was promised, declaring that the dragon has three heads and that there must be a third child.
notes: Seen in the House of the Undying. Rhaegar may have been wrong about which Aegon — or the three heads may be Daenerys, Jon, and another. Rhaegar's obsession with prophecy is what led him to Lyanna Stark.

title: Winter Is Coming
description: The Stark house words function as both a practical warning and a prophecy. They foretell the coming of a great winter and the Long Night — the existential threat from beyond the Wall.
notes: Unlike other house words that are boasts, the Stark words are a warning. They function on multiple levels: literal (winter is approaching), metaphorical (hard times ahead), and prophetic (the Others are coming).

title: Fire and Blood
description: House Targaryen's words serve as both their identity and a promise of what Daenerys will bring to Westeros — conquest through dragonfire.
notes: The words are both a historical reference to Aegon's Conquest and foreshadowing of Daenerys's planned invasion of Westeros.

title: Mirri Maz Duur's Promise
description: Mirri Maz Duur tells Daenerys that Khal Drogo will return to her when the sun rises in the west and sets in the east, when the seas go dry, when the mountains blow in the wind like leaves — in other words, never.
notes: Mirri intended this as 'never,' but readers note potential fulfillments: Quentyn Martell (the sun's son from the west) dies in the east; the Dothraki Sea is drying. Daenerys's fertility may be restored, subverting Mirri's intended meaning.

title: The Stallion Who Mounts the World
description: The Dothraki dosh khaleen prophesy that Daenerys's unborn son will be the Stallion Who Mounts the World — a great conqueror who will unite all the Dothraki and ride to the ends of the earth.
fulfillment_evidence: Daenerys's son Rhaego is stillborn, killed by Mirri Maz Duur's blood magic. The prophecy of the Stallion Who Mounts the World dies with him — or perhaps is transferred to Daenerys herself, who goes on to conquer with her dragons.
notes: One of GRRM's clearest prophecy subversions. The prophesied savior is killed before birth. Some argue Daenerys herself becomes the 'stallion' figure.

title: The Bleeding Star and Salt and Smoke
description: Multiple prophecies state that Azor Ahai will be reborn amidst salt and smoke beneath a bleeding star. Different characters claim different events fulfill this — Daenerys's dragons born on a funeral pyre under the red comet, or Stannis at Dragonstone.
notes: Maester Aemon on his deathbed argues for Daenerys: born in salt (the sea) and smoke (Drogo's pyre) under a bleeding star (the red comet). Jon Snow's assassination at the Wall is also proposed — salt from Bowen Marsh's tears, smoke from his wounds steaming in the cold.

title: Bran's Vision of the Heart of Winter
description: Through the weirwood network, Bran has a terrifying vision of the far north — the Land of Always Winter — where he sees the heart of winter itself. He glimpses what lies at the core of the Others' domain.
notes: One of the earliest and most mysterious visions in the series. The 'heart of winter' has never been explained. It may be the key to understanding and defeating the Others.

title: The Ghost of High Heart: A Shadowed Man
description: The Ghost of High Heart dreams of a man without a face waiting on a bridge, and of a shadow with a burning heart killing a golden stag.
fulfillment_evidence: The faceless man on the bridge is Jaqen H'ghar, encountered by Arya at the Twins region. The shadow with a burning heart is Melisandre's shadow assassin (bearing the sigil of R'hllor) that kills Renly Baratheon (the golden-antlered stag).
notes: The burning heart is the sigil of R'hllor, specifically associated with Stannis and Melisandre. The 'golden stag' is Renly with his crown.

title: The Rains of Castamere as Foreshadowing
description: The song 'The Rains of Castamere' recounts Tywin Lannister's total destruction of House Reyne. Throughout the story, it plays before acts of Lannister violence — most notably at the Red Wedding, where the band begins playing it as the signal for the massacre.
fulfillment_evidence: The song plays at the Red Wedding as the signal for the slaughter. Its recurring presence throughout ASOS foreshadows Lannister-orchestrated violence. Cersei also uses the song to intimidate Margaery Tyrell.
notes: Functions as narrative foreshadowing rather than a prophecy per se. The song itself is a warning about what happens to those who defy the Lannisters.

title: The Weirwood Dreams of Ned's Promise
description: Ned Stark has recurring fever dreams about the Tower of Joy — Lyanna making him promise something, a bed of blood, blue winter roses. The narrative surrounds Lyanna's words with mystery and dread.
notes: Not a prophecy in the traditional sense but heavy narrative foreshadowing of Jon Snow's true parentage. The recurring blue roses connect to the House of the Undying vision of a blue flower in the Wall.

title: The House of the Undying: The Silver Horse
description: In the House of the Undying, Daenerys sees a vision of a beautiful woman being ravished by four little men, representing the suffering of the common people at the hands of the warring kings.
fulfillment_evidence: The beautiful woman represents Westeros or the common people, and the four 'dwarfs' represent the kings of the War of the Five Kings tearing the realm apart for their own ambitions.
notes: One of the allegorical visions from the House of the Undying. The four men correspond to the warring claimants devastating the realm.

title: The House of the Undying: The Stone Beast
description: Daenerys sees a great stone beast taking flight from a smoking tower, breathing shadow fire. This vision appears as one of the lies she must slay.
notes: Interpretations vary widely. The stone beast may represent Stannis (Dragonstone), Jon Connington at Griffin's Roost, or even a literal stone dragon. The 'shadow fire' suggests something false or corrupted.

title: The House of the Undying: The Corpse at the Prow
description: Daenerys sees a corpse standing at the prow of a ship with bright eyes and a grey, smiling face. The vision is associated with the title 'bride of fire.'
notes: The smiling corpse at the prow of a ship has been linked to Euron Greyjoy, Victarion, or even a resurrected Jon Snow. The 'bride of fire' context suggests this figure may be a future husband or suitor for Daenerys.

title: The House of the Undying: Rhaegar at the Trident
description: Daenerys sees a tall lord with copper skin and silver-gold hair dying in a stream, rubies scattering from his chest. He whispers a woman's name with his last breath.
fulfillment_evidence: The vision depicts Rhaegar Targaryen's death at the Battle of the Trident, where Robert Baratheon's warhammer scattered the rubies from Rhaegar's breastplate. The name he whispers is likely Lyanna.
notes: This vision shows the past rather than the future. The woman's name is almost certainly Lyanna Stark, reinforcing R+L=J. The ruby ford at the Trident is named for this event.

title: Moqorro's Vision of Victarion
description: The red priest Moqorro tells Victarion Greyjoy that he has seen him in the flames — a great captain with a dark hand, surrounded by glory. He warns that the iron captain must beware a tall and twisted thing with one black eye and ten long arms, sailing on a sea of blood.
notes: The twisted thing with one black eye and ten arms is almost certainly Euron Greyjoy (who wears an eye patch). Moqorro warns Victarion of his own brother's treachery, but Victarion may not heed the warning.

title: Moqorro's Vision of Dragons
description: Moqorro tells Tyrion that he has seen dragons in the flames — old and young, true and false, bright and dark. He says one dragon stands above the rest, casting a long shadow across the world.
notes: The 'true and false' dragons likely refer to Daenerys (true) and Young Griff (false). The dragon casting a long shadow may be Drogon or Daenerys herself. This vision reinforces the mummer's dragon theme.

title: Melisandre's Vision: Daggers in the Dark
description: Melisandre warns Jon Snow that she has seen daggers in the dark and that he should keep Ghost close. She senses mortal danger gathering around him at the Wall.
fulfillment_evidence: Jon Snow is stabbed by his own Night's Watch brothers in a mutiny, attacked with daggers in the darkness at Castle Black. He had sent Ghost away, ignoring Melisandre's warning.
notes: One of Melisandre's most accurate and correctly interpreted visions. Jon dismisses her warning, partly because he distrusts her motives. His refusal to keep Ghost nearby proves fatal.

title: Melisandre's Vision: Towers by the Sea
description: Melisandre sees towers by the sea, submerged in a black and bloody tide. She interprets this as a vision connected to Eastwatch-by-the-Sea, warning of a threat from that direction.
notes: The identity of the towers is debated — they could represent Eastwatch-by-the-Sea or another coastal stronghold. The black and bloody tide may herald an Other assault or a naval attack.

title: Theon's Dream of the Feast of the Dead
description: While captive at Winterfell, Theon dreams of a great feast in Winterfell's hall. The dead sit at the tables — Robb Stark, Grey Wind, and others killed at the Red Wedding — all watching him with accusation.
fulfillment_evidence: Many of the figures Theon sees at the ghostly feast — including those who served Winterfell — are later killed during Theon's betrayal, the sack of Winterfell, or the Red Wedding. The dream foreshadows the mass death that follows.
notes: The dream reflects both foreshadowing and guilt. The dead feast may also echo Norse mythology's einherjar — warriors feasting in the halls of the dead.

title: Daenerys's Vision in the Dothraki Sea
description: Alone and fevered in the Dothraki Sea after fleeing Meereen on Drogon, Daenerys has hallucinations where she is visited by a vision of Quaithe urging her to remember who she is. She sees the ghosts of the Targaryen past and resolves to embrace fire and blood.
notes: This vision marks a turning point in Daenerys's arc. She decides to stop compromising and embrace her destiny as a conqueror. The hallucinated Quaithe repeats her earlier warnings and guidance.

title: Cersei's Dream of Tyrion
description: Cersei has a recurring nightmare in which Tyrion is hiding beneath her bed or in the walls, coming to kill her in the darkness. The dream reflects her paranoia about the valonqar prophecy.
notes: Cersei's dreams about Tyrion are driven by her obsession with Maggy the Frog's prophecy. Her paranoia about her little brother leads her to see threats everywhere, contributing to her political downfall.

title: Bran's Weirwood Vision: The Heart Tree Crying
description: When Bran touches the weirwood roots in the cave of the Three-Eyed Crow, he experiences visions spanning centuries — he sees a woman praying to the heart tree, sacrifices made before it, and the carved face of the tree weeping red sap like tears of blood.
notes: Bran's weirwood visions show that the greenseer ability allows perception across time, not just space. The weeping face connects to the idea that the old gods — or past greenseers — observe and grieve through the trees.

title: Bran's Weirwood Vision: Seeing Through Time
description: Through the weirwood network, Bran sees fragmentary visions of Winterfell's past — a young woman and a tall man who may be his father, a sacrifice in the godswood, and other scenes spanning thousands of years.
notes: Bloodraven tells Bran that the greenseers can see through the weirwood trees but cannot change the past. However, Bran seems to make Ned sense his presence at one point, suggesting the rules may not be absolute.

title: The Woods Witch and the Prince That Was Promised
description: A woods witch prophesied to the court at King's Landing that the prince that was promised would be born from the line of Prince Aerys and Princess Rhaella. This led King Jaehaerys II to insist on their marriage.
notes: The woods witch is believed to be the same person as the Ghost of High Heart. This prophecy forced the unwilling marriage of Aerys and Rhaella, ultimately producing both Rhaegar and Daenerys — two prime candidates for the prince that was promised.

title: The Dragon Must Have Three Heads
description: Rhaegar declares that the dragon must have three heads, believing three Targaryens are needed to fulfill the prophecy and ride the three dragons. This drives his search for a third child.
notes: The three heads are most commonly theorized as Daenerys, Jon Snow, and a third (possibly Tyrion). Rhaegar's belief that he needed a third child may have motivated his relationship with Lyanna Stark.

title: Aemon's Realization About the Prince
description: On his deathbed, Maester Aemon realizes that Daenerys may be the prince that was promised, noting that the Valyrian word for 'prince' has no gender. He declares that the error was assuming the prophecy referred to a man.
notes: Aemon's insight challenges generations of assumption. High Valyrian is described as having gender-neutral terms, meaning 'the prince' could equally be 'the princess.' Aemon dies regretting that he is too old and frail to go to Daenerys.

title: Jojen's Greendream: His Own Death
description: Jojen Reed appears to know the manner and time of his own death, stating that the day of his death is not today. His growing melancholy north of the Wall suggests he knows his end approaches.
notes: Jojen becomes increasingly despondent in the cave of the Three-Eyed Crow, and Meera tells Bran that Jojen knew they would never leave. The 'Jojen paste' theory suggests his fate is darker than implied.

title: Patchface: The King's Blood in the Snow
description: Patchface sings of a fool with a crown and the king's blood running in rivers of red through the white snow. He dances while singing of death and sacrifice.
notes: The 'king's blood' in the snow may foreshadow a royal sacrifice — possibly Shireen's burning, which has been heavily foreshadowed. Melisandre's repeated interest in king's blood and her proximity to Shireen is deeply ominous.

title: Ghost of High Heart: The Maiden in the Tower
description: The Ghost of High Heart describes seeing a maiden in a high tower, with a dwarf below casting a long shadow that reached across the land. The dwarf devoured the maiden whole.
fulfillment_evidence: The maiden in the tower is Sansa Stark in the Eyrie, and the shadow-casting dwarf is Littlefinger (whose seat is the smallest holding in the realm). Littlefinger manipulates Sansa and essentially 'devours' her identity as he reshapes her into Alayne Stone.
notes: The 'dwarf' is metaphorical — referring to Littlefinger's humble origins, not his physical stature. His shadow reaching across the land represents his outsized political influence.

title: We Do Not Sow
description: House Greyjoy's words encapsulate the Ironborn philosophy of taking what they want through strength rather than creating it. The words foreshadow the Ironborn's role as raiders and destroyers throughout the War of the Five Kings.
fulfillment_evidence: The Ironborn fulfill their words throughout the series — Theon seizes Winterfell, Euron raids the Reach, and the Ironborn repeatedly take by force what others have built. Their philosophy drives much of the destruction in the Riverlands and North.
notes: Unlike the Stark warning or Targaryen promise, the Greyjoy words are a statement of cultural identity. They describe what the Ironborn are, and in doing so, predict their destructive role in the narrative.

title: Unbowed, Unbent, Unbroken
description: House Martell's words express their unconquered status — the only kingdom never subjugated by Aegon's Conquest. The words foreshadow Dorne's resistance to outside control and their independent scheming.
fulfillment_evidence: Dorne maintains its independence and pursues its own agenda through Doran Martell's secret plans for a Targaryen alliance. However, their schemes suffer setbacks — Quentyn dies, Oberyn falls, and Myrcella is maimed.
notes: The Martell words have an ironic edge — while Dorne remains politically independent, their plans are repeatedly broken by fate. Doran's patient schemes crumble one by one.

title: Ours Is the Fury
description: House Baratheon's words serve as a promise of their wrath. The fury of Robert's Rebellion, Stannis's relentless campaigns, and Renly's bold claim all embody these words in different ways.
fulfillment_evidence: Robert unleashed his fury to overthrow the Targaryens. Stannis brings fire and fury to his campaign for the Iron Throne. The Baratheon fury ultimately turns inward as the three brothers destroy each other's legacies.
notes: The Baratheon words prove prophetic in a tragic sense — the fury consumes the house itself. By the end of ADWD, all three Baratheon brothers are dead or presumed doomed.

title: Dragon Eggs as Physical Signs
description: The three petrified dragon eggs given to Daenerys as a wedding gift are ancient, believed to have turned to stone over the centuries. Their warmth and the way they seem to respond to heat foreshadow the hatching to come.
fulfillment_evidence: The eggs hatch on Drogo's funeral pyre, producing Drogon, Rhaegal, and Viserion — the first dragons seen in over a century. Daenerys's growing connection to the eggs and their warmth in her hands foreshadows this moment.
notes: The eggs are described as warm to the touch when Daenerys holds them, and she places them in a brazier to warm them. These physical details serve as signs that the dragons are not truly dead inside.

title: The Cold Winds Rising
description: Multiple characters sense or warn that the cold winds are rising — an ominous phrase repeated throughout the series that signals the approach of the Others and the coming Long Night.
fulfillment_evidence: The Others are increasingly active throughout the series — killing rangers, raising wights, and massing beyond the Wall. The phrase serves as a drumbeat of warning that the supernatural threat is growing.
notes: The phrase appears in various forms from multiple characters. It functions as both literal meteorological observation and prophetic warning about the approaching existential threat.

title: Euron's Dragonbinder Horn
description: Euron Greyjoy presents a massive Valyrian horn called Dragonbinder at the Kingsmoot, claiming it can bind dragons to the will of its master. The horn kills the man who blows it, his lungs charring from within.
notes: The horn bears Valyrian glyphs that translate roughly to 'I am Dragonbinder... No dragon can resist me.' Euron sends it with Victarion to Meereen, but Moqorro may be helping Victarion claim it for himself. The horn's true effect on dragons remains untested.

title: The Horn of Joramun
description: Legend holds that the Horn of Joramun, a mythical artifact, can bring down the Wall. Mance Rayder claims to have found the horn, and the wildlings believe blowing it would shatter the Wall's magical foundations.
notes: The horn Mance displays is a decoy. The real Horn of Joramun may be the broken horn that Sam carries, found at the Fist of the First Men with the obsidian cache. If the Wall falls, it would be the most catastrophic fulfillment in the series.

title: Theon's Dream of the Miller's Boys
description: After killing the miller's boys and passing them off as Bran and Rickon, Theon dreams of the dead children accusing him. Their faces shift between the miller's boys and the Stark children, blurring the line between his victims.
fulfillment_evidence: The dream reflects Theon's guilt and foreshadows his mental breakdown. The dead boys haunt him because he knows his deception — Bran and Rickon live, but the miller's boys are truly dead by his hand.
notes: Theon's dreams foreshadow his transformation into Reek. The psychological torment from this moment is the beginning of his descent, which Ramsay Bolton later completes.

title: Jon's Dream of the Crypts of Winterfell
description: Jon Snow has recurring dreams of wandering the crypts beneath Winterfell. He knows there is something down there waiting for him but is afraid to go deeper. The Kings of Winter watch him with stone eyes.
notes: The crypts may hold the secret of Jon's parentage — possibly Rhaegar's harp, a marriage cloak, or other evidence of Lyanna's marriage. Jon's fear of what waits below mirrors his reluctance to confront his true origins.

title: Daenerys's Dream of the Ice Threat
description: Before hatching her dragons, Daenerys dreams of standing against a great darkness coming from the far north — a wall of ice and shadow that threatens to consume the world. She breathes fire to hold it back.
notes: This dream establishes Daenerys's potential role in the fight against the Others, connecting the fire/ice dichotomy. It suggests that her dragons may be needed not for Westerosi politics but for the existential threat beyond the Wall.

title: The Old Bear's Raven
description: Lord Commander Mormont's raven repeatedly croaks words that seem prescient — 'king,' 'snow,' 'corn,' and 'burn.' The bird's utterances often coincide with important decisions or revelations at the Wall.
notes: Many readers believe the raven is being warged or influenced by Bloodraven (Brynden Rivers), the Three-Eyed Crow. Its repeated cries of 'king' and 'snow' near Jon may be deliberate attempts to guide events.

title: Old Nan's Tales of the Long Night
description: Old Nan tells Bran stories of the first Long Night — when the Others came with cold and darkness that lasted a generation, and the last hero went out with his companions to seek the children of the forest.
notes: Old Nan's stories are dismissed by most characters as fairy tales, but they consistently prove to contain truth. Her accounts of the Others, giants, and the children of the forest all turn out to be real, suggesting her Long Night narrative is also accurate.

title: The Damphair's Vision of Euron on the Iron Throne
description: Aeron Greyjoy has a terrifying vision of his brother Euron seated on the Iron Throne, a dark and monstrous figure with a great shadow rising behind him, presiding over a feast of horrors.
notes: Aeron's vision portrays Euron as an apocalyptic figure, far more dangerous than a mere pirate king. The Damphair fears Euron on a primal level, sensing something deeply unnatural about his brother.

title: The Valyrian Doom as Precedent
description: The Doom of Valyria — a cataclysmic volcanic event that destroyed the greatest civilization in history — serves as a recurring warning throughout the narrative. The Targaryens survived only because Daenys the Dreamer foresaw it in a vision twelve years before.
fulfillment_evidence: The Doom is a historical fact in the story — confirmed by multiple sources. Daenys's prophetic dream that saved House Targaryen demonstrates that dragon dreams can be genuinely prophetic, lending credibility to Daenerys's own visions.
notes: The Doom of Valyria establishes that prophetic dreams run in the Targaryen bloodline and can be devastatingly accurate. It also serves as a warning about the hubris of great civilizations.

title: Varys's Riddle of Power
description: Varys poses a riddle to Tyrion: a king, a priest, and a rich man each command a sellsword to kill the others. The question of who lives and who dies depends on who the sellsword believes has power — illustrating that power resides where people believe it resides.
fulfillment_evidence: The riddle is proven true throughout the series as power shifts constantly. The crown, the faith, and gold all compete for supremacy, and each succeeds or fails based on belief and perception rather than inherent right.
notes: Not a prophecy but a philosophical framework that predicts the series' power dynamics. The Faith Militant's rise, Cersei's fall, and the collapse of Lannister authority all illustrate Varys's point about the nature of power.

title: The Three-Eyed Crow's Call
description: After his fall, Bran is visited in his coma dreams by a three-eyed crow that tells him he must fly or die. The crow pecks at his forehead, opening his third eye to greensight.
fulfillment_evidence: Bran does learn to 'fly' — through warging and greensight. The three-eyed crow is revealed to be Brynden Rivers, the last greenseer, who has been calling to Bran through dreams to bring him north for training.
notes: The crow's command to fly or die is both metaphorical (embrace his powers) and literal (he is in a coma and might die). The opening of his third eye represents the awakening of his supernatural abilities.

title: Melisandre's Vision: Skulls and Patchface
description: When Melisandre looks at Patchface in her flames, she sees skulls piling around him and hears the sound of distant screaming. She considers the fool to be more dangerous than any of Stannis's human enemies.
notes: Melisandre's terror of Patchface is one of her most visceral reactions. Given that Patchface is often near Shireen, this vision may foreshadow a catastrophic event involving both of them — possibly Shireen's sacrifice.

title: The Catspaw Dagger
description: A Valyrian steel dagger is sent to kill Bran Stark after his fall. The dagger's origins and the identity of who sent the assassin become central mysteries, with the weapon itself serving as a physical sign of the conspiracy tearing the realm apart.
fulfillment_evidence: The dagger sets the entire plot in motion — Catelyn's investigation leads to Tyrion's arrest, which triggers the War of the Five Kings. The weapon is a physical catalyst for the realm's destruction.
notes: Littlefinger falsely identifies the dagger as Tyrion's to drive a wedge between Stark and Lannister. The true sender is debated — likely Joffrey acting on a misunderstood comment from Robert about mercy-killing cripples.

title: Cersei's Prophecy Obsession
description: Cersei's entire political strategy in AFFC is shaped by her obsessive fear of Maggy the Frog's prophecy. She sees the younger queen, the valonqar, and threats to her children everywhere, making increasingly paranoid decisions that bring about her own downfall.
fulfillment_evidence: Cersei's paranoia about the prophecy leads her to arm the Faith Militant, alienate the Tyrells, and make catastrophically poor political decisions — ironically bringing about the very downfall the prophecy predicted.
notes: A masterful example of a self-fulfilling prophecy. Cersei's attempts to prevent the prophecy's fulfillment are precisely what cause it to come true. Her fear of Margaery leads her to empower the Faith, which destroys her own power.

title: Daenerys's Vision of the Undying's Warnings
description: The Undying whisper warnings to Daenerys about three fires she must light (for life, for death, for love), three mounts she must ride (to bed, to dread, to love), and three treasons she must face (for blood, for gold, for love).
fulfillment_evidence: The fire for life may be Drogo's pyre that hatched the dragons. The mount to bed may be her silver horse. The first treason for blood may be Mirri Maz Duur. The remaining elements are still unfolding.
notes: This prophecy overlaps with the Daughter of Death vision but focuses on the specific numerical pattern. The repetition of threes is central to Daenerys's arc and connects to the three-headed dragon motif.

title: Maester Aemon's Warning About Prophecy
description: Maester Aemon warns that prophecy is like a treacherous woman — it can appear to mean one thing while meaning something entirely different. He cautions against placing too much trust in prophetic visions.
fulfillment_evidence: Nearly every prophecy in the series has been misinterpreted by at least one character. Melisandre misreads her visions. Cersei misidentifies the younger queen. Rhaegar may have been wrong about which child was the prince that was promised.
notes: Aemon's meta-commentary on prophecy serves as a guide for readers. GRRM uses prophecy as a literary device that is always technically accurate but frequently misleading, rewarding careful reading and punishing overconfidence.

title: Jon Snow's Dream of Armored in Black Ice
description: Jon dreams of standing atop the Wall alone, armored in black ice with a burning red sword in his hand. Below him, the dead are massing, their blue eyes shining in the darkness.
notes: This dream strongly parallels Azor Ahai imagery — a burning sword against the forces of darkness. The 'black ice' armor may symbolize Jon's dual nature (Stark ice and Targaryen fire) or his role as a defender at the Wall.

title: The Septon's Prophecy of the Stone Dragon
description: Melisandre seeks to wake a stone dragon on Dragonstone by sacrificing king's blood. The concept of waking stone dragons connects to multiple prophecies about Azor Ahai and the return of dragons to the world.
fulfillment_evidence: Melisandre believes she can literally wake the stone dragon statues on Dragonstone through blood sacrifice, but Davos prevents the sacrifice of Edric Storm. Daenerys may have already 'woken stone dragons' — her petrified eggs.
notes: Another example of Melisandre correctly identifying a prophecy element but misunderstanding how it is fulfilled. The 'waking of stone dragons' already happened at Drogo's pyre. Melisandre's literal interpretation nearly leads to an unnecessary sacrifice.

title: The Alchemists' Wildfire
description: The Alchemists' Guild reports that they are producing wildfire faster and in greater quantities than expected, with their spells working better than they have in years. This correlates with the return of dragons to the world.
fulfillment_evidence: The strengthening of fire magic coincides directly with the hatching of Daenerys's dragons. Magic is returning to the world, and fire-based magic in particular is growing stronger — the glass candles burn again, the pyromancers' spells improve.
notes: The return of magic is a physical sign that the world is changing. Wildfire, glass candles, and fire visions all grow stronger after the dragons are born, suggesting a deep connection between dragons and magic itself.
